title: A Ruinous Government and a Few Newspapers Singing Its “Bhajans”!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation and terminal date omitted by OCR have been restored, the corrupted project value has been checked against the printed page, and clear OCR word-break and symbol errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Union Government has repeatedly charged that the Tamil Nadu Government does not provide the cooperation needed to complete highway projects. Nitin Gadkari, the new Union Highways Minister, who visited Tamil Nadu two days ago, has now expressed the same concern.

I discussed this in detail as early as 14 August 2013, under the title “Can the State Government Refuse What the Union Government Offers?”, when the United Progressive Alliance was still in office at the Centre.

Soon after the DMK assumed office in 2006, I wrote to Prime Minister Manmohan Singh on 5 June requesting approval for an elevated expressway linking Chennai Port with Maduravoyal. The Union Government approved it, and Prime Minister Manmohan Singh personally laid its foundation stone on 8 January 2009 under my leadership as Chief Minister.

When reporters asked Union Surface Transport Minister Oscar Fernandes in 2013 what had happened to this important four-lane elevated road, he replied: “Banks were refusing to finance the project in the absence of clear-cut information from the State Government about its stand and a delay in clearing encroachments.”

In a petition to the High Court, the National Highways Authority's project director said work began after approvals were obtained, but cooperation from State departments disappeared after the change of Government. The State did not assist with land acquisition or rehabilitation, for reasons that were unclear, and showed no interest in the project. He asked the court to direct the State to cooperate so the work could continue and be completed on schedule.

Dina Thanthi itself reported that the Rs 1,815 crore Port-Maduravoyal Expressway, intended to reduce Chennai's congestion, faced abandonment. The contractor sought Rs 872 crore in additional compensation for delays, pushing the National Highways Authority towards dropping the project. Exporters and importers would suffer and Chennai Port's revenue would collapse.

Container trucks from southern districts now reach the port through the bypass, Maduravoyal, Madhavaram, Puzhal and Hundred Feet Road. Trucks from northern districts travel through the Manali refinery road and Ennore Highway. Congestion around Minjur and Madhavaram forces them to crawl. At times a truck takes two days merely to reach the port from Manali Highway, wasting both time and fuel. The Port-Maduravoyal expressway is the proper answer to all these problems. NHAI officials said work would continue only when favourable conditions returned.

The National Highways Authority said it was the State Government's “motivated, tainted” action that had stalled the work. The Hindu reported that no new highway project could be sanctioned in Tamil Nadu until the State signed and honoured its cooperation agreement.

A Chennai Port official said: “The stoppage of Maduravoyal Project is ruining us. We will be losing crores of rupees every year.” Former Union minister G. K. Vasan likewise said the State had blocked the project, although it was vital to both the port and the city. The Union Government had repeatedly requested its acceleration, and the Union Secretary had recently discussed it with the Chief Secretary.

The High Court noted that Rs 1,000 crore had already been spent on this major project inaugurated by the Prime Minister. It accepted the Union Government's case that the new Tamil Nadu Government had deliberately obstructed construction. The court allowed work to continue and directed the State to cooperate fully without creating impediments.

It is in this setting that Union Shipping Minister Nitin Gadkari asked the Tamil Nadu Government for greater cooperation on infrastructure. NHAI chief general manager V. Chinnappa Reddy said the Authority was ready to undertake five new road projects covering 407.26 kilometres at a cost of Rs 1,840 crore. Two more four-lane projects could be taken up if land acquisition reached ninety per cent. The State had the crucial role: the Centre was ready to build, but needed greater State cooperation in acquiring land and removing encroachments.

The announced projects are: Tirumayam-Manamadurai, 78 kilometres, Rs 252 crore; Thanjavur-Pudukkottai, 55 kilometres, Rs 159 crore; Karaikudi-Ramanathapuram, 80 kilometres, Rs 280 crore; Madurai-Ramanathapuram, 116 kilometres, Rs 734 crore; and Nagapattinam-Thanjavur, 79 kilometres, Rs 415 crore. There are also two four-lane proposals, between Kanniyakumari and Kavalkinaru, and along Vikravandi-Kumbakonam-Thanjavur. With proper cooperation from the Tamil Nadu Government, all could be completed quickly with Union funding.
After reporting these details, today's Times of India, dated 6 November 2014, carried further disturbing information under the headline “Infra Shutdown Slows Down State.”

National highway projects worth Rs 25,000 crore remain stalled. These include the Port-Maduravoyal elevated road, Ennore-Manali road, Chennai-Tirupati highway, Chennai-Tada highway, Tiruchi bypass, and Krishnagiri-Walajapet highway. They have not begun because the State keeps advancing artificial objections.

Power projects worth Rs 38,800 crore remain unstarted: the 660 MW Ennore thermal project; 1,600 MW Udangudi project; 1,600 MW Uppur project; 800 MW North Chennai project; 1,200 MW North Chennai Special Economic Zone project; and 660 MW Ennore expansion.

Another Rs 5,000 crore in national highway proposals has been prevented from moving beyond the concept stage. Repeated Union appeals that the State abandon confrontation and cooperate have produced no result.

The closure of the giant Nokia factory exposes the State Government's hostility to industrial growth. Because the Port-Maduravoyal road remains unfinished, car manufacturers face serious problems. Cars being exported through the port must wait there for more than forty-eight hours, wasting money and time.

Tamil Nadu's allocation for infrastructure keeps shrinking. While the Indian average is seven to eight per cent, Tamil Nadu spends only five per cent. Consequently, in 2012-13 the State's growth rate fell to 3.39 per cent, against the national average of 4.4 per cent.

Retired IAS officer Devasahayam said political interference had greatly increased the time needed even to begin a project. Industrialists found it nearly impossible to meet former Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, making decisions impossible.

Plans had been made to meet investors from around the world in October and attract Rs 1 lakh crore, but the meeting has still not occurred. Jayalalithaa's conviction further paralysed the administration. Although another Chief Minister has taken office, industrial growth has received no impetus.

Udanpirappē, all these details appeared in a leading English newspaper. Yet Tamil newspapers conceal them. Seeking to protect the AIADMK Government and those running it, they turn even their breathing into a banner headline spread across eight columns, while suppressing opposition views.
So long as there is a Government that thinks, “What does it matter how badly Tamil Nadu is ruined?”, and a few newspapers that sing “bhajans” in its praise, this is how things will continue.
With affection,
M.K.
